coredata.dc:title: Decorating red-light-emissive, N-doped carbon dots on bismuth sulfide to promote the photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Linwen
Hou, Shida
Li, Penghui
Zhou, Shilu
Zhang, Shuming
Li, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description:
Searching for photocatalysts with wide absorption range and high efficiency to solve the growing pollution issues such as organic pollutant degradation is vitally necessary for the upcoming clean and renewable energy systems. In this work, we employed one-step hydrothermal reaction to prepare bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) incorporated with N-doped, red-light-emissive carbon dots (r-CDs) which manifest effective interface contact and excellent photocatalytic performance. The introducing of the r-CDs, which could be excited by long wavelength lights, efficiently widens the range of light absorption of the hybrid material and improves the ability of electron transfer, accounting for the remarkably higher photocatalytic efficiency of the hybrid material toward the degradation of methylene blue. A possible mechanism behind the efficient photocatalytic activity of the r-CDs/Bi2S3 is proposed where electrons transfer from Bi2S3 to the r-CDs for accelerating the separation of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. During the photocatalytic reaction, h
+ , •
OH and •
O
2
− simultaneously participated, as demonstrated by active species trapping experiments. This work provides a pace for designing high-performance photocatalysts based on the introducing of r-CDs with long wavelength emission, which is promising for catalytic and new energy applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped
Red-light-emissive
Carbon dot
Bismuth sulfide
Photocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structures and properties of the photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Fund of Shandong University
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Synthetic procedure of r-CDs. b,c) HRTEM image and the size distribution of r-CDs. d,e) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the r-CDs in EG (0.02 mg mL−1).
SEM image (a), TEM and HRTEM images (b), EDX (c) and element mapping images (d) of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4.
Raman spectra (a), XRD patterns (b), TGA curves (c) and FTIR spectra (d) of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4. Results of r-CDs and Bi2S3 are also shown wherever necessary for comparison.
XPS spectra of r-CDs, Bi2S3 and r-CDs/Bi2S3-4: full survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution N 1s (b, c) and C 1s (d–f) spectra.

a) Photocatalytic activities of r-CDs/Bi2S3 with different content of r-CDs toward degradation of MB (10 mg L−1) under visible light irradiation for 60 min b) Variation of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the MB with time at the presence of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4. c) I–t curves of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 and pure Bi2S3 at − 0.3 V (vs. SCE). d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 and pure Bi2S3.
Illustration of the proposed mechanism of the charge separation in r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid photocatalyst under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Decorating red-light-emissive, N-doped carbon dots on bismuth sulfide to promote the photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Etsong Tobacco (Group) Co., Ltd., Qingdao 266011, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Searching for photocatalysts with wide absorption range and high efficiency to solve the growing pollution issues such as organic pollutant degradation is vitally necessary for the upcoming clean and renewable energy systems. In this work, we employed one-step hydrothermal reaction to prepare bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) incorporated with N-doped, red-light-emissive carbon dots (r-CDs) which manifest effective interface contact and excellent photocatalytic performance. The introducing of the r-CDs, which could be excited by long wavelength lights, efficiently widens the range of light absorption of the hybrid material and improves the ability of electron transfer, accounting for the remarkably higher photocatalytic efficiency of the hybrid material toward the degradation of methylene blue. A possible mechanism behind the efficient photocatalytic activity of the r-CDs/Bi2S3 is proposed where electrons transfer from Bi2S3 to the r-CDs for accelerating the separation of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. During the photocatalytic reaction, h
+ , •
OH and •
O
2
− simultaneously participated, as demonstrated by active species trapping experiments. This work provides a pace for designing high-performance photocatalysts based on the introducing of r-CDs with long wavelength emission, which is promising for catalytic and new energy applications.

Bi2S3-based photocatalysts were prepared which are incorporated with highly dispersed, N-doped r-CDs, which exhibits high photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of MB under the illumination of visible light.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysts for degradation of organic pollutants using solar light energy have attracted increasing interest in recent years due to the growing environment concerns and the energy demand [1,2]. Unfortunately, most of the semiconductors with a wide band gap can be only excited by ultraviolet (UV) light that accounts for only 4% of the solar energy [3]. To efficiently utilize the solar energy, ideal photocatalyst should have a wide range of response spectrum covering the visible and infrared lights, effective charge separation and migration ability. The idea of forming a heterojunction structure between a photocatalyst and a narrow bandgap semiconductor with matched band potentials may provide a way to address these issues [4]. However, this strategy is largely hampered by choosy semiconductor materials.

Among various semiconductors, bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) with a bandgap of ~1.3 eV has received considerable attention in photocatalysis because of its unique electric and optical properties [5,6]. Specifically, Bi2S3 shows photocatalytic capability for degrading methylene blue dye (MB) under light irradiation [7]. However, the slow rate of charge transfer and the fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes restrict its photocatalytic activity. To lower the rate of recombination, Bi2S3 has been coupled with other metal-based materials such as BiOCl [8] and Fe3O4 [9]. However, the efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation of target organic compound is still poor.

Carbon dots (C dots), comprising a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial with dimensions typically below 10 nm, have attracted widespread interest due to their ease of preparation, tunable structures and high photostability [10–14]. C dots have been integrated with semiconductors to improve the photocatalytic activity [15]. However, most of the C dots utilized to date show emission in the blue to green-light regions under excitation of UV light, which limits their photocatalytic applications [16]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to achieve red-light-emissive C dots (r-CDs) which can be excited under long wavelength light irradiation.

The r-CDs demonstrated in the literatures suffer from relatively low fluorescent quantum yield (φ) and complicated synthetic routes [17]. To overcome this defect, doping with nitrogen element could be an effective way to promote electron transfer and to expand the region of the light absorption [18]. To the best of our knowledge, so far, there is no report on the preparation of Bi2S3-based hybrid materials which are incorporated with N-doped r-CDs.

Herein, we synthesized Bi2S3 nanotubes which are decorated with uniformly dispersed, N-doped r-CDs through a one-step hydrothermal reaction. The so-obtained r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid exhibits efficient photoactivity under visible light, which could decompose as high as 96% of MB within 30 min. The catalyst was characterized in detail to elucidate how the mechanism behind the high photocatalytic activity. It is found that the introducing of the r-CDs dots promoted the charge separation and transfer between Bi2S3 and r-CDs dots. A variety of intermediates including h
+ , •
OH and •
O2
− simultaneously participated in the photocatalytic reaction, accounting for the enhancement of the photocatalytic efficiency.

Urea (AR, 99.0%) and formanide (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Sodium oxalate (AR, 99.5%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Bimuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3•5H2O, AR, 99.0%), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S•9H2O, AR, 98.0%) and p-benzoquinone (AR, 97.0%) were purchased from Aladdin industrial Co. Citric acid (AR, 99.0%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Organic solvents including formamide, ethylene glycol (EG) and acetone were purchased from Tianjin FuYu chemical co., LTD (China) with AR grade. All reagents were used without further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm prepared from a UPH-IV ultrapure water purifier (China) was used throughout the experimental process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out on a JEM-100CX II (JEOL) operating at 80 kV with a Gatan multiscan CCD recording the images. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a HRTEM JEOL 2100 system with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) observations were carried out on Hitachi SU8010 under 10 kV. The diffuse-reflectance spectra were obtained on a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, ISA-220 accessory, Thermo Scientific) using a built-in 10 mm silicon photodiode with a 60 mm Spectralon sphere. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a SDTQ600 thermogravimetric analyzer under a N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1. FTIR spectra were obtained on a VERTEX-70/70 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatized Al K X-ray source (1486.71 eV). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15418 nm. The concentration of MB in the system during the reactions was monitored by UV-Vis spectrophotometry (HR2000 + CG-UV-NIR, Ocean Optics). Raman spectra were acquired using a Renishaw in Via Reflex Confocal Raman Microscope with an excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm.

The N-doped r-CDs were synthesized through pyrolysis process following the reported procedures with modifications [19–21]. In a typical experiment, citric acid monohydrate (60 g) and formamide (200 mL) were added to a round-bottom with vigorous stirring until the solid was dissolved totally. The mixture was then refluxed for 15 min. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was precipitated by five times acetone, filtered and dialyzed with a Snake Skin Pleated Dialysis Tubing membrane (1000 Da) against water for several days. Finally, the r-CDs powder was obtained by lyophilizing the as-prepared aqueous stock solution.
For comparison, yellow-emissive CDs (y-CDs) have also been prepared. Details of the synthesis can be found in our previous reports [22,23].

Pure Bi2S3 was prepared through a straightforward hydrothermal method following procedures reported in the literature [24]. The r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid was prepared using the same method at the presence of r-CDs. In brief, to an EG solution of Bi(NO3)3•5H2O (0.146 g mL−1, 5 mL) was added a desired amount of r-CDs. The mixture was stirred by a magnetic stirrer for 30 min, followed by the addition of aqueous solution of Na2S•9H2O (0.108 g mL−1, 10 mL). Then, aqueous solution of urea (0.0513 g mL−1, 15 mL) was slowly added and the mixed solution was further stirred for 30 min. The black mixed solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged and washed with water for several times until the upper solution is clear and transparent. Finally, the collected sample was lyophilized to remove water. Totally four hybrid were prepared with the amount of added r-CDs to be 5, 25, 50 and 100 mg, respectively. The samples were successively named as r-CDs/Bi2S3-1, r-CDs/Bi2S3-2, r-CDs/Bi2S3-3 and r-CDs/Bi2S3-4. The photocatalyst of y-CDs/Bi2S3 was prepared following the same processes by replacing r-CDs by y-CDs. The amount of the added y-CDs was fixed at 100 mg.

A CHI 660B electrochemical workstation was used to study the photoelectrochemical activities with conventional three-electrode setup under visible-light illumination. A platinum wire and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode were employed as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively, and a 0.2 mol L−1 aqueous solution of Na2S•9H2O was used as electrolyte (pH 6.8). The working electrodes were prepared by drop-coating sample suspensions directly onto the pre-cleaned, fluorine-doped tin oxide glass (FTO glass) surface. In detail, powder of the catalysts (2 mg) was dispersed into 1 mL water containing 10 μL Nafion under ultrasonic treatment for 60 min 50 μL sample mixture was drop-coated onto the FTO glass and dried in oven at 60 ℃. The detailed photocurrent response of as-prepared photoelectrodes were measured under a chopped irradiation from 300 W high-pressure short-arc xenon lamp as illumination source at room temperature.

A 350 W Xe lamp was utilized as the visible light source in the photocatalytic process. The catalyst was put into 8 mL aqueous solution of MB (10 mg L−1) and stirred for 30 min in dark to reach adsorption equilibrium before exposed to the visible light. At regular intervals, 1 mL solution was quickly transferred into a quartz cuvette and analyzed via UV–vis absorption. The dye degradation efficiency was calculated by the following formula:
Efficiency
( % )
=
A
0
− A
A
0
× 100 %
Where A0 and A were the maximum absorbance at 665 nm before and after photocatalytic process.

The synthetic procedure of N-doped r-CDs is presented in Fig. 1a, which belongs to the “bottom-up” strategy where small organic molecules are carbonized into CDs upon pyrolysis [25]. The selected carbon source is citric acid and the solvent is formamide. After being refluxed for 15 min, the product was purified by precipitation with five times acetone by volume followed by dialysis with a dialysis tubing membrane (1000 Da) against water (for details, see experimental section). The as-prepared sample emits red light under 365 nm UV irradiation, indicating the successful preparation of the r-CDs. TEM observation showed that the r-CDs have an average diameter of ~6.5 nm (Fig. 1b and c). The optical properties of the r-CDs were thoroughly investigated. As shown in Fig. 1d, the r-CDs exhibit a wide absorption range from 100 to 1000 nm with a sharp peak at 550 nm and a shoulder one around 377 nm, which can be assigned to n–π* and π–π* transitions, respectively [26]. Fluorescence measurements showed that the emission of r-CDs is quite broad and contain multiple emission maxima, which indicates that they have complicated internal structures and rich surface states [27]. The emission are excitation-dependent with a maximum intensity observed at an excitation wavelength of 460 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and properties of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the obtained photocatalysts were investigated in detail, and typical results are summarized in Figs. 2 and S1, S2. Pure Bi2S3 are nanotubes (Fig. S1). With increasing amounts of r-CDs, the morphology of the hybrid material changed gradually to nanosheets. From HR-TEM image of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 (Fig. 2b), the lattice spacing of 0.33 nm agrees with the (211) crystal plane of Bi2S3 [28]. It can be seen that the r-CDs, which appear as black spots, have been effectively loaded on the surface of Bi2S3. However, the r-CDs are mainly non-crystalline, and lattices could hardly be observed. EDX spectrum and elements mapping are used to further demonstrate the successful preparation of the r-CDs/Bi2S3, in which the uniform distribution of Bi, S, C and N has been confirmed (Fig. 2c and d). From the analysis on the element contents (Tables S1 and S2), we can directly see that the N element is present in the photocatalyst. This observation proves the successful incorporation of the r-CDs as they are known to contain N due to the participation of formamide during the pyrolysis [19–21].

The structures of the photocatalyst were further characterized by a variety of techniques using r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 as a typical example. In Raman spectrum ( Fig. 3a), mainly three peaks were observed for r-CDs/Bi2S3-4. Peaks of 1614 and 1363 cm−1 are attributed to the sp2 carbon networks (G band) and the disorder or defects in the graphitized structure (D band) of N-doped r-CDs sample, respectively [29]. The peak of 973 cm−1 is attributed to Bi2S3. In the spectrum of r-CDs, peaks of 967, 1097, 1268, 1493 and 1562 cm−1 in are ascribed to pyridine-breathing, pyrrole-ring breathing, C‒C, C‒O‒C and N˭N signals, respectively, further demonstrating the doping of N atoms [20,30]. In XRD patterns (Fig. 3b), peaks from r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 are consistent with those of pure Bi2S3, both of which are perfectly ascribed to the orthorhombic structure of Bi2S3 (JCPDS card no. 17–0320) [31]. This result means that there is no structural transformation with the introducing of r-CDs. However, there is no obvious peak from r-CDs in r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 because of the low content of r-CDs. From TGA curves (Figs. 3c, S3), ~4.9% weight loss was detected for r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 around 600 ℃, which can be ascribed to the partial decomposition of incorporated r-CDs as there is negligible weight loss for pure Bi2S3 at the same temperature range. In FT-IR spectrum of r-CDs (Fig. 3d), peaks at 1640, 1562, 1347 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C˭N/C˭O, C˭C and C–N, respectively [32]. Peaks at 1190, 1093 cm−1 can be attributed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C‒O‒C. Peaks at 3391 and 3186 cm−1 represent the amino and hydroxyl functional groups, and those at 3010 and 2785 cm−1 come from C‒H stretching vibrations of methyl/methylene [33]. The FT-IR spectrum of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 shows the same characteristic with that of r-CDs, confirming that r-CDs have been successfully hybridized with Bi2S3.

The chemical composition and valences of each element of the photocatalyst were further investigated by XPS. The full survey spectrum ( Fig. 4a) verifies that Bi, S, C, N and O elements co-exist in r-CDs/Bi2S3-4. Notably, the emergence of N peak at 399.6 eV is attributed to the decorated r-CDs. The N1s peak of the r-CDs (Fig. 4b) could be fitted into two peaks located at 399.5 and 400.2 eV, assigning to the pyrrolic-like N and graphitic-like/amino N, respectively [34]. When incorporated into Bi2S3, both the positions and ratio of these two peaks remain almost unchanged (Fig. 4c). The C1s spectrum of r-CDs (Fig. 4d) could be divided into five peaks, i.e., 284.3 eV (C˭C/C‒C), 285.1 eV (C‒N), 286.1 eV (C‒O), 287.5 eV (C˭N/C˭O), and 288.4 eV (‒COOH) [35]. These peaks were also detected in r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 (Fig. 4e), confirming the successful preparation of r-CDs/Bi2S3 photocatalyst. In comparison, no peaks of C‒N and C˭N/C˭O were observed in pure Bi2S3 (Fig. 4f) due to the absence of the r-CDs. The C and O elements in pure Bi2S3 come from the added urea which was used as mineralizer in the hydrothermal process. The O 1s spectrum of the r-CDs can be divided into two fitting peaks at 531.0 and 532.0 eV which are attributed to C˭O and C‒OH/C‒O‒C bonds (Fig. S4), indicating that the r-CDs are decorated with amino and hydroxyl functional groups, according with the FTIR and Raman spectra. High-resolution spectra of Bi and S showed that the peaks of these two elements remained unchanged before and after the incorporation of r-CDs (Fig. S5), indicating that the basic structure of Bi2S3 was not disturbed which is consistent with the results from XRD.

The photocatalytic behavior of r-CDs/Bi2S3 toward the degradation of MB were evaluated under AM 1.5 G illumination with non-sacrificial reagents at room temperature. For comparison, the performance of pure Bi2S3 was also evaluated under the same condition. Fig. 5a shows the photocatalytic ability of different photocatalysts. Pure Bi2S3 shows quite limited photocatalytic activity with efficiency of only ~23% after 60 min. The positive effects of the incorporation of r-CDs on the photocatalytic activity is obvious. With increasing amount of r-CDs, the photocatalytic efficiency continuously increased. The values after 60 min were determined to be ~28% for r-CDs/Bi2S3-1, ~61% for r-CDs/Bi2S3-2, ~78% for r-CDs/Bi2S3-2 and ~98% for r-CDs/Bi2S3-4, respectively. One can see that the sample with the highest amount of r-CDs, i.e., r-CDs/Bi2S3-4, exhibits the optimal photocatalytic activity. The change of the UV–vis absorption of MB as a function of time during the photocatalytic reaction is shown in Fig. 5b, which indicated that the concentration of MB decreased rapidly to ~18% of its initial value within the first 5 min. The concentration further reduced to as low as 4% of its initial value after 30 min of light illumination. After continuous irradiation for 60 min, the initially blue solution turned colorless, indicating that the remaining MB is negligible.

In order to the emphasize the important role of r-CDs, another type of CDs which emit yellow light were also evaluated (this type of CDs are denoted as y-CDs hereafter). Using a similar procedure, the y-CDs could also be incorporated into Bi2S3. However, the y-CDs/Bi2S3 with the same amount of y-CDs as r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 shows much poorer photocatalytic activity compared to r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 under the same experimental condition. As shown in Fig. S6, the efficiency of MB degradation is only ~50% within the first 5 min, which moderately increased to ~56% after 60 min. These results demonstrate that the incorporation of r-CDs endows the hybrid photocatalyst with an enhanced photocatalytic ability. Besides the wider optical window of the r-CDs compared to y-CDs, we suppose that the larger content of N element of r-CDs compared to that of y-CDs (0.73% by elemental analysis [22], also see Fig. S7) also plays a role.

To further confirm the efficient charge separation as well as electron transfer, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) tests have been performed [36,37] As shown in Fig. 5c, the I-t curves clearly confirmed that r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 exhibited higher photocurrent density than that of pure Bi2S3, revealing its more efficient charge separation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also performed to explore the charge transfer process, where r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 with the smallest arc radius exhibited remarkably increased interfacial charge transport efficiency (Fig. 5d). These results clearly demonstrate the highly efficient photogenerated charge separation and transfer over r-CDs/Bi2S3-4, which are highly consistent with the observed superior photocatalytic activity.

To further clarify the mechanism behind the excellent photocatalytic activity of the r-CDs/Bi2S3 photocatalyst, different trapping agents were used in the active species trapping experiments to reveal their roles during the photocatalytic degradation process [38,39]. The photocatalytic activity of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 toward the degradation of MB with or without trapping agents are summarized in Fig. S8. Notably, the photocatalytic activity of r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 evidently reduced when sodium oxalate (h
+ acceptor), isopropanol ( •
OH trapper) and p-benzoquinone ( •
O
2
−) were added, indicating that the h
+ , •
OH and •
O
2
− must play important roles in the photodegradation process, which are indispensable for promoting the photocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, the electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap technique was carried out to investigate the active species generated by r-CDs/Bi2S3-4 under the visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S13, the sample showed a central symmetry absorption peak centered at 329.4 mT under the light irradiation, which is ascribed to the unpaired electron and free electron [40,41], corresponding with the active species trapping experiments.

As shown in Fig. S9, the band gap (Eg) of direct band gap semiconductors was estimated from the Tauc’s equation (αhv = A(hv-Eg)
1/2 ), where α, h, ν, A, and Eg represent the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively [42]. The estimated band gap of the Bi2S3 is 1.25 eV. From the valence band XPS spectra shown in Fig. S10, the valence band of Bi2S3 is 0.73 eV. Thus, the conduction band of Bi2S3 is calculated to be − 0.52 eV according to the formula of E
CB
= E
VB
– Eg. The reduction potentials of •
OH/OH
- and
O
2
/ ⋅
O
2
−
are known to be 2.38 and − 0.046 eV vs. NHE, respectively. Therefore, it was speculated that Bi2S3 easily trap O
2 to generate
⋅
O
2
−
but the holes (h
+ ) cannot oxidize OH
- to form •
OH. According with the trapping experiments, •
OH radicals could be generated by further reduction of
⋅
O
2
−
. More specifically, under light irradiation, the electron-hole pairs were generated and the photo-generated electron efficiently transfer from Bi2S3 to N-doped r-CDs for photocatalytic reactions, and the holes stayed in the valence band (VB) of Bi2S3 hampering the recombination of electron/hole pairs, which could be rapidly consumed for degradation reaction. Based on these analysis, band positions and photogenerated charge transfer process for the r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid photocatalyst are proposed and given in Scheme 1.

In summary, we have developed a facile hydrothermal method to synthesize Bi2S3-based photocatalysts which are incorporated with highly dispersed, N-doped r-CDs. Various characterizations have been employed to confirm the successful preparation and the structural details of the r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid. The photocatalytic activity of the r-CDs/Bi2S3 hybrid was evaluated, applying the degradation of MB as a model reaction. Benefiting from the synergistic effect between r-CDs and Bi2S3, the hybrid exhibited efficient visible light photoactivity. Active species trapping experiments indicated that •
OH,
⋅
O
2
−
and h
+ simultaneously participated in photocatalytic reactions. This work highlights the crucial role played by the N-doped r-CDs in improving the performance of the semiconducting photocatalyst, which can be a useful guide for developing highly efficient photocatalysts in future.

Linwen Zhang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shida Hou: Investigation. Penghui Li: Investigation. Shilu Zhou: Investigation. Shuming Zhang: Investigation. Hongguang Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129) and The Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (No. 2018TB033). This work was also financially supported by the joint project between Shandong University and China Tobacco Shandong Industrial Co., Ltd. (No. JL-JS01029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126397.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterojunction of facet coupled g-C3N4/surface-fluorinated TiO2 nanosheets for organic pollutants degradation under visible LED light irradiation
Appl. Catal. B Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Dai, L. Lu, C. Liang, Q. Liu, G. Zhu, Heterojunction of facet coupled g-C3N4/surface-fluorinated TiO2 nanosheets for organic pollutants degradation under visible LED light irradiation, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 156‐157 (2014) 331‐340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Science and technology for water purification in the coming decades
Nature
